title: Roys Wedding

jokes.text: A wheel is supposed to spin.
jokes.setup: Pam creates a non-spinning 'chore wheel' system
jokes.punchline: Simple, logical objection to the fundamental flaw
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and logic - exposes the absurdity of Pam's system with minimal words

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Wait, if it doesn't spin, how is it a wheel?

jokes.text: guh-guh-guh-guh-guh-guh-guh-guh-guh-h-guh...
jokes.punchline: Elaborate vocal spinning sound effect, interrupted by Pam's dismissal
jokes.explanation: Childish but effective - the extended sound effect and Pam's deadpan interruption create good contrast

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Wheels should spin.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Whirrrrrrrrrr... whirrrrrrrr... whirrrrrrrr...

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: They do spin.

jokes.text: Yuck. Boring. All she talks about is chores.
jokes.punchline: Dismissive, childish complaint about grown-up responsibilities
jokes.explanation: Standard workplace comedy - contrasts adult responsibility with childish whining

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: So you have to file the quarterly reports, update the client database, and make sure the expense reports are submitted by Friday.

dialogue.speaker: Unknown character
dialogue.line: But I don't wanna do grown-up stuff!

jokes.text: A wheel wants to spin, Pam.
jokes.punchline: Anthropomorphizes the wheel with earnest conviction
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist logic delivered with perfect sincerity - anthropomorphizing inanimate objects is classic comedy

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: The wheel has feelings, Jim. You're hurting it.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Dwight, it's a wheel.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: A wheel with dreams. Dreams you're crushing every time you spin it like that.

jokes.text: Big money, big money.
jokes.setup: Wheel now spins like a game show
jokes.punchline: Game show contestant enthusiasm for office chores
jokes.explanation: Solid reference humor - brings game show energy to mundane office task

dialogue.speaker: Michael Scott
dialogue.line: Alright everyone, gather around! We're going to make this fun. I'm going to spin this wheel and whatever chore it lands on, that person has to do it with the enthusiasm of a game show contestant!

dialogue.speaker: Dwight Schrute
dialogue.line: I'm ready. I have the stamina of a game show contestant.

dialogue.speaker: Michael Scott
dialogue.line: Okay, here we go... *spins wheel* ...Dwight, you got... filing!

dialogue.speaker: Dwight Schrute
dialogue.line: FILING?! *jumps up excitedly* OH MY GOD, I LOVE FILING! This is the best day of my life! What a prize! What a wonderful, wonderful prize!

jokes.text: This sucks. Yeah, seriously, it's like everything on there's work.
jokes.punchline: Dawning realization that chores are still work
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of excitement - obvious realization delivered with appropriate disappointment

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Alright everyone, gather around! We're going to make chores fun with this spinning wheel!

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: A spinning wheel? What are we, at a carnival?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: No, Jim. We're making work... fun. You spin the wheel, it tells you what chore to do.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: That actually sounds kind of cool.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I'm ready to take on any assignment with the utmost dedication.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Okay, here we go! *spins wheel* And it lands on... clean the break room!

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Wait, so we still have to do chores?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Well, yes, but—

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: It's still just work.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: But... the wheel makes it fun.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Not really, Michael. It's still cleaning.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Chores are chores, sir. No amount of spinning changes that.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: *stares at wheel, deflated*

jokes.text: Yeah, and I don't think that you understand wheels.
jokes.setup: Pam says they don't understand the purpose
jokes.punchline: Escalates to fundamental wheel philosophy
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation - takes the wheel argument to philosophical extremes with perfect confidence

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: We don't understand the purpose.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: The purpose of the wheel is to move things forward.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: But what if we don't want things to move forward?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Then you're arguing against the fundamental philosophy of the wheel itself.

jokes.text: The one thing that is not on the chore wheel is chores, but they were right. It's more fun this way.
jokes.setup: Pam's talking head about wheel development
jokes.punchline: Admits removing chores from the chore wheel
jokes.explanation: Perfect Pam moment - practical manager learns to embrace chaos through design iteration

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: I may have taken some things off the chore wheel. Like, all of them. I have just been doing them myself.

jokes.text: Tiny wheel! Tiny wheel!
jokes.punchline: Enthusiastic chanting for miniature version
jokes.explanation: Good physical escalation - the tiny wheel becomes an object of group excitement

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Yes! Yes! Yes! Come on, come on, come on!

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: What is he doing?

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: I think he's trying to win something.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Miniature! Miniature! Miniature!

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Why is he chanting miniature?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Yes! A tiny Beet farm! This is the greatest day of my life!

jokes.text: The tiny wheel actually does have chores.
jokes.setup: Tiny wheel is cute and popular
jokes.punchline: Reveals it's the only one with actual work
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection - the cutest option contains the actual responsibility

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Okay, so this tiny wheel is cute and popular.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: But it turns out it's the only one with actual work.

jokes.text: It's so cute, no one seems to mind.
jokes.punchline: Psychology of cute presentation overriding content
jokes.explanation: Sharp observation about presentation vs. content - very Pam's managerial insight

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Okay, so Tiny Wheel has chores.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: But look how cute it is when it's presented that way. I mean, you can't say no to that.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: It's literally just a chore wheel.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: But it's a TINY chore wheel. The cuteness is overriding the actual content.

jokes.text: Toilets! Aww.
jokes.punchline: Collective 'aww' at disgusting chore like it's adorable
jokes.explanation: Perfect execution of the cute packaging concept - toilet cleaning gets baby voice treatment

dialogue.speaker: Multiple characters
dialogue.line: Aww...

jokes.text: I think the only weird thing about going to your wife's ex-fiancee's wedding on a weekday at 8 AM is that it's your wife's ex-fiancee.
jokes.setup: Lists multiple weird aspects of the situation
jokes.punchline: Dismisses logistics, focuses on core weirdness
jokes.explanation: Classic Jim observation - builds up minor details then lands on the obvious main issue with perfect timing

jokes.text: Come on, not even Roy will have hot dogs at his wedding. I planned a wedding with him. He wanted hot dogs.
jokes.setup: Jim defends Roy's wedding food choices
jokes.punchline: Pam's experience contradicts with specific example
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup/punchline with authentic character history - Jim's optimism meets Pam's reality

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Roy's a great guy. The food at his wedding was amazing.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Jim, the entire appetizer table was just different types of meat on sticks.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Exactly. Meat on sticks. It was a sophisticated affair.

jokes.text: Well, it could be a nice letter. I write nice letters to companies all the time. That's really nice.
jokes.punchline: Erin's naive optimism and Pete's awkward politeness
jokes.explanation: Good character contrast - Erin's sweetness against Pete's resignation, with awkward social exchange

dialogue.speaker: Pete
dialogue.line: I hate dealing with customer service emails. People are just so angry all the time.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Oh, I think they're great! Everyone's just trying their best. Maybe they're having a bad day and your email will make them smile!

dialogue.speaker: Pete
dialogue.line: Erin, they're complaining about a broken toaster.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: So tell them you'll fix it with the power of friendship!

dialogue.speaker: Pete
dialogue.line: That's... that's not going to work.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Not with that attitude!

jokes.text: This election thing is crazy, right?
jokes.setup: Pete trying to make conversation with Newsweek
jokes.punchline: Desperately generic political small talk
jokes.explanation: Authentic social awkwardness - Pete's attempt at intellectual connection falls flat

dialogue.speaker: Pete
dialogue.line: So, uh, crazy weather we've been having, huh? Really something else. I mean, politics these days, am I right? Just nuts. Anyway, how about those sports teams? They're really... doing things. You follow any of them? Yeah, me too. Well, not really, but I feel like I should.

jokes.text: Yeah. I accidentally read it. It's not a nice letter.
jokes.punchline: Pete's deadpan admission and understatement
jokes.explanation: Good callback and character work - Pete's practical deflation of Erin's optimism

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: I love getting nice letters. It just makes my whole day!

dialogue.speaker: Pete
dialogue.line: Yeah, I got a letter once. It was my eviction notice. That was... not nice.

jokes.text: Andy has shot down all of my special projects thus far, but this one is about charity so I'd like to see him piss on that one.
jokes.punchline: Vulgar challenge about opposing charity
jokes.explanation: Good character voice - Nellie's manipulative strategy with crude imagery

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: I'm starting a charity initiative to help underprivileged children.

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: If anyone opposes this, they can go f*** themselves.

jokes.text: Would you like me to take your peel?
jokes.punchline: Overly formal service for banana peel
jokes.explanation: Simple class contrast - fancy service for mundane trash

dialogue.speaker: Dwight Schrute
dialogue.line: I have prepared an elegant seven-course meal service.

dialogue.speaker: Jim Halpert
dialogue.line: Dwight, is that a banana peel on the plate?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight Schrute
dialogue.line: Yes. A banana peel deserves the utmost respect and formal presentation. I have arranged the finest linens, the most exquisite china, and a dedicated server for each course.

dialogue.speaker: Pam Beesly
dialogue.line: That's just a banana peel, Dwight.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight Schrute
dialogue.line: In the Schrute family, we treat all foods with dignity, regardless of their peel status.

jokes.text: I started a new business with my college friend, but Pam doesn't know. Um, actually I... I did tell Pam, and we decided no, but then I decided 'yes, anyway,' so I'm thinking there's another conversation coming, and it's hard to know when that will be.
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's defying joint marital decision
jokes.explanation: Strong character development - Jim's confession reveals marital tension with awkward honesty

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I'm telling you something I probably shouldn't. Pam and I made a pact that we wouldn't tell anyone at work, but... I'm going to tell you anyway.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: We decided together that we wouldn't say anything about this, but honestly, I don't care. She's going to be so mad.

jokes.text: Listen to this. They give a poor person like a goat or something. It's a great prank.
jokes.punchline: Frames helping people as a prank
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ryan - completely misunderstands charity concept as elaborate joke on recipients

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: Heifers International is this amazing charity where you can donate a heifer to a poor family, and it totally changes their life.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: It's like the ultimate prank. You give someone a cow and their whole world is different.

jokes.text: I wanna work with, uh, Jimmy Carter and Help Build Gnomes.
jokes.punchline: Confuses 'Homes' with 'Gnomes' in Habitat for Humanity
jokes.explanation: Classic Kevin malapropism - garden gnomes instead of houses, delivered with complete confidence

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: I'm going to support Habitat for Gnomes.

dialogue.speaker: Someone
dialogue.line: It's Habitat for Humanity.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: That's what I said.

jokes.text: I will not be participating, as there is no evidence that charity works.
jokes.punchline: Dwight's pseudo-scientific rejection of all charity
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight logic - applies scientific skepticism to basic human kindness

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I don't believe in charities. Billions of dollars are wasted on people who don't deserve it. Poverty is nature's way of telling you that you've failed at life.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: The only charity I support is the kind that helps strong people become stronger. That's Dwight Schrute's charity.

jokes.text: I give to a foundation that teaches homeless children nautical flag signaling... Changes Lives.
jokes.punchline: Completely impractical skill for homeless children
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist charity - combines Andy's privileged background with completely useless skill

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'm very charitable. I donate to a lot of causes. Last week I donated to a program that teaches homeless children juggling.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: It's a completely impractical skill for homeless children, but I thought, 'Hey, at least they'll have something to do while they're starving.'

jokes.text: I'm not doing it for you. I'm doing it for the preservation of nautical flag signaling.
jokes.punchline: Andy's passionate defense of maritime communication
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation - Andy treats maritime signals like an endangered species

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Andy, why do you care so much about maritime communication systems?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Because Dwight, the sea is a harsh mistress and those sailors out there depend on reliable communication to survive! You think it's funny? You think it's a joke? These are real people risking their lives every single day, and someone has to stand up for their right to proper radio protocols and maritime safety standards!

jokes.text: Fine, I choose the Global Relief Foundation... which was recently discovered to be a front for the Taliban!
jokes.punchline: Chooses terrorist front organization
jokes.explanation: Peak Dwight malicious compliance - finds the one charity worse than no charity

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Dwight, you need to pick a charity for the office to support.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I have selected the perfect organization. The Ku Klux Klan.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Dwight, no! That's not a charity, that's a hate group!

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: What? I thought it stood for 'Klans Keeping Kids Keen on Learning.'

jokes.text: The Taliban is the worst. Great heroin, though.
jokes.punchline: Creed's casual drug knowledge qualification
jokes.explanation: Perfect Creed moment - inappropriate expertise delivered with casual authority, excellent economy

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: I'm not a drug guy. I just know a lot about them.

jokes.text: The nanny state is over, ladies and gentlemen. You're welcome.
jokes.punchline: Treats charity blocking as liberation
jokes.explanation: Classic Dwight political rhetoric applied to office charity - treats kindness as oppression

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I'm not blocking the charity initiative. I'm liberating this office from the tyranny of forced compassion.

jokes.text: Come on, if it wasn't for you, I never would've met Laura. But seriously, could've dodged a bullet on that one. Just kidding.
jokes.punchline: Jokes about dodging bullet marrying Pam
jokes.explanation: Awkward wedding humor - Roy's inappropriate joke about his ex at his wedding

dialogue.speaker: Roy
dialogue.line: Thanks for everything you guys did.

dialogue.speaker: Roy
dialogue.line: I dodged a bullet not marrying Pam.

jokes.text: I started a gravel company. I mean, who knew it would take off?
jokes.punchline: Surprise at gravel company profitability
jokes.explanation: Good character development - blue-collar Roy finds unexpected business success

dialogue.speaker: Roy
dialogue.line: You know what? I got out of the warehouse, started my own gravel company. Best decision I ever made.

dialogue.speaker: Roy
dialogue.line: People always need gravel. It's a recession-proof business.

dialogue.speaker: Roy
dialogue.line: Last year I made more money than I ever did at Dunder Mifflin.

dialogue.speaker: Roy
dialogue.line: Turns out there's a lot of money in rocks.

jokes.text: Not gravel, obviously.
jokes.punchline: Jim's obvious clarification about not competing
jokes.explanation: Jim's awkward social navigation - unnecessarily clarifies the obvious

dialogue.speaker: Roy
dialogue.line: What do you do?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I'm in sales. I sell paper.

dialogue.speaker: Roy
dialogue.line: So you're like a competitor?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: No, no. I work here. At Dunder Mifflin. I'm not competing with you.

jokes.text: He's got a $50,000 sports car.
jokes.setup: Roy dismissing Jim to talk to Kenny
jokes.punchline: Kenny's inappropriate wealth disclosure
jokes.explanation: Solid awkward moment - inappropriate financial bragging at social event

jokes.text: I got a buddy that's a big time local news producer, and I can't tell you his name, but it'd blow your mind. Is it Duncan?
jokes.setup: Clark builds mystery about unnamed contact
jokes.punchline: Erin immediately guesses correctly
jokes.explanation: Good setup/punchline - Clark's dramatic secrecy immediately deflated by Erin's guess

dialogue.speaker: Clark
dialogue.line: I've been talking to someone. They're really important to me, but I can't tell you who they are yet.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Is it Dwight?

dialogue.speaker: Clark
dialogue.line: How did you—

jokes.text: You'll just come over, put on a little lipstick, loosen up with a glass of red wine, and then we'll just run through a few stories... with a couple different outfits.
jokes.punchline: Increasingly inappropriate elements (wine, outfit changes)
jokes.explanation: Effective cringe - professional facade gradually reveals predatory intentions

dialogue.speaker: Clark
dialogue.line: So I'm doing this professional news audition, right? And it starts off normal - I'm reading the teleprompter, nailing it.

dialogue.speaker: Clark
dialogue.line: Then halfway through, someone hands me a glass of wine. I'm like, okay, this is a test of professionalism. I keep going.

dialogue.speaker: Clark
dialogue.line: Next thing I know, they're telling me to change outfits. So I'm doing quick changes between every segment. Still reading the news.

dialogue.speaker: Clark
dialogue.line: By the end, I'm half-naked, three glasses of wine deep, and I'm reporting on the stock market like it's a late-night comedy bit.

dialogue.speaker: Clark
dialogue.line: Didn't get the job.

jokes.text: Clark's not my friend. He is the douche that sits next to me at the office. My friends are Scott, Glenn and Rob, but you don't know them.
jokes.punchline: Defensive clarification about real friends
jokes.explanation: Good character voice - Erin's need to establish she has real friends elsewhere

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Clark is a real friend. Like, we hang out outside of work and everything. We go to the movies, we get lunch... I mean, yeah, okay, I pay for everything, and he only texts me when he needs something, but that doesn't mean he's not a real friend. That just means he's a guy.

jokes.text: I mean, what if the ad had been for a CEO or for a brain surgeon?
jokes.setup: Erin questioning why she's just a receptionist
jokes.punchline: Believes job applications are random assignments
jokes.explanation: Perfect Erin logic - doesn't understand qualifications, treats careers as random chance

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: I don't understand why I'm just a receptionist. I have so many skills and talents.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: I mean, I applied to be a lot of different things. How did they just pick receptionist?

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: I think job applications are just random. Like, they throw all the applications up in the air and whoever lands on top gets the job.

jokes.text: I thought she was my waitress, and, uh, took her three weeks to tell me that she actually owned the place.
jokes.setup: Roy describing how he met Laura
jokes.punchline: Misidentified restaurant owner as waitress for weeks
jokes.explanation: Solid character story - reveals Roy's lack of observation skills in romantic context

dialogue.speaker: Roy
dialogue.line: Yeah, so I met Laura at this restaurant, right? And I kept asking her for refills, asking her to take my order, the whole thing. Turns out she owned the place.

jokes.text: He plays piano? No. Roy? No.
jokes.setup: Roy about to perform musical surprise
jokes.punchline: Pam and Jim's incredulous responses
jokes.explanation: Good character reaction - Pam's intimate knowledge of Roy's limitations

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Wait, Roy's doing what?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I think he's about to sing.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Roy doesn't sing.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: No, he really doesn't.

jokes.text: You know how I said I was taking boxing lessons? Actually, I was doing this
jokes.punchline: Reveals secret piano practice instead
jokes.explanation: Nice misdirection - tough guy Roy secretly learning romantic skills

dialogue.speaker: Roy
dialogue.line: Yeah, I've been going to boxing lessons after work.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Boxing lessons? Since when?

dialogue.speaker: Roy
dialogue.line: Few weeks now. Gotta stay in shape, you know?

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: That's actually really cool, Roy.

dialogue.speaker: Roy
dialogue.line: Yeah, well... *pauses* ...okay, it's not boxing.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: What do you mean?

dialogue.speaker: Roy
dialogue.line: I've been taking piano lessons. Classical piano. Don't tell anyone.

jokes.text: Yes, but what I didn't tell you is that I actually bought the tickets. We only didn't go because it was... It was an away game. In Phoenix.
jokes.setup: Jim mentions wanting to surprise Pam with tickets
jokes.punchline: Reveals he bought wrong tickets due to misunderstanding
jokes.explanation: Great escalation - Jim's romantic gesture becomes confession of incompetence

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I wanted to surprise Pam with tickets to that concert she's been talking about.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Turns out I bought tickets to a Metallica show instead of Matchbox Twenty.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: She's not really into heavy metal, so... that didn't go over well.

jokes.text: They should really tell you that more specifically. I mean, every game is an away game for one of the teams.
jokes.punchline: Doubles down with illogical complaint about terminology
jokes.explanation: Excellent Jim logic - attempts to rationalize obvious mistake with philosophical observation

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I bought tickets to the wrong concert.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Which concert?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I don't know, some band called 'The Who.' But here's the thing — if they're called 'The Who,' shouldn't they have to tell you who they are before you buy tickets?

jokes.text: Did I ever tell you about the time that my brothers videotaped the lottery announcement and bought the winning numbers the next day, and then, played the tape for me the next week... And you thought you guys were millionaires. You heard that one.
jokes.punchline: Pam knows the story, deflating the moment
jokes.explanation: Good couple dynamic - Jim's attempt to share story deflated by Pam's knowledge

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: So there was this one time when I was in Scranton, and I had just gotten back from—

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Sandals Jamaica, 2003, you got food poisoning.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: ...Yeah.

jokes.text: That you thought you were millionaires. That's funny. That's all right.
jokes.punchline: Awkward forced appreciation
jokes.explanation: Authentic couple awkwardness - polite but forced interaction after failed storytelling

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: So then I said to the client, 'That's what she said.'

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Get it?

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Yeah, I got it.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: It was pretty funny.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Mmhmm. Really funny.

jokes.text: The senator and I still have mystery. I'm always waiting to see what he's gonna surprise me with next.
jokes.setup: Angela comparing her relationship to Jim/Pam
jokes.punchline: Ironic given senator's closeted homosexuality
jokes.explanation: Dark dramatic irony - Angela's comment is loaded with unintended meaning for viewers

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: My relationship with Mark is just like Jim and Pam's. Ironic, given the senator's closeted homosexuality.

jokes.text: You support the Taliban abroad, so I assume you're willing to live by their rules here.
jokes.punchline: Nellie demands logical consistency
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation - Nellie traps Dwight with his own logic

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. You chose to donate to a charity that's a Taliban front? That's... that's not acceptable.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: It was the most efficient use of resources.

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: Dwight, you can't just pick whichever charity is most efficient if it's funding terrorists! There has to be some moral consistency here.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Morality is subjective.

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: No, it isn't! Not about this!

jokes.text: Absolutely, I will.
jokes.punchline: Dwight's immediate enthusiastic agreement
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight - never backs down from extreme position, regardless of consequences

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: I challenge anyone here to name one law the Taliban has that's better than ours.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I can't.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: But I admire their can-do attitude.

jokes.text: I feared Nelly had some sort of a plan, but she used a ridiculous font. You don't have a plan! When you use a ridiculous font, no one thinks you have a plan.
jokes.punchline: Font choice as indicator of competence
jokes.explanation: Excellent Dwight observation - treats typography as strategic intelligence

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Nellie uses Comic Sans. That tells you everything you need to know about her competence as a manager. Comic Sans is the font of the weak-minded. A strong leader uses Arial or Times New Roman. Preferably in bold.

jokes.text: This is a lovely pen, and it's mine now, because I stole it.
jokes.punchline: Casual theft confession as legal demonstration
jokes.explanation: Good escalation - Nellie turns Dwight's extreme position into practical trap

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: I'm implementing Taliban law here at Dunder Mifflin. For example, if you steal, your hand gets cut off.

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: I stole a pen from Office Depot last week. So technically, I should lose this hand.

jokes.text: That means you're not serious, or someone's getting their hand cut off.
jokes.setup: Taliban law requires punishment for theft
jokes.punchline: Nellie demands extreme enforcement
jokes.explanation: Strong escalation - forces Dwight to confront logical extreme of his position

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: Under Taliban law, anyone caught stealing must have their hand cut off.

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: I demand we enforce that here. Immediately.

jokes.text: will have to chop off my hand.
jokes.setup: Dwight won't back down from Taliban law
jokes.punchline: Accepts hand amputation rather than admit error
jokes.explanation: Peak Dwight stubbornness - would rather lose limb than admit mistake

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I will not back down from Taliban law. If they say my hand must be amputated, then so be it. I will not admit error.

jokes.text: I suggest you spend some quality time together, just you and your hand, tie a shoe, toss a salad. Do any of the two-hand activities that you'll miss the most.
jokes.punchline: Morbid farewell activities with sexual innuendo
jokes.explanation: Dark humor with innuendo - Nellie's practical approach to amputation preparation

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: Well, if I'm going to lose my hand, I might as well make it memorable. I'm thinking a final farewell tour—shake everyone's hand one last time. And then maybe something a little more... hands-on.

jokes.text: Something low-cut, because the camera makes everything seem higher-cut. Really? Yeah, industry secret.
jokes.punchline: Fake 'industry secret' about cleavage
jokes.explanation: Solid cringe - Clark's transparent manipulation with fake expertise

jokes.text: And then, you know, maybe just a robe to wear in between takes, but I probably got one that she could borrow at my place, so...
jokes.punchline: Robe at his apartment suggestion
jokes.explanation: Effective cringe escalation - Clark's predatory plan becomes more obvious

jokes.text: Thank God someone here knows what they're talkin' about.
jokes.punchline: Andy's oblivious praise for expertise
jokes.explanation: Good dramatic irony - Andy completely misses Clark's obvious scheming

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Clark, I have to say, your ability to make completely inappropriate requests while maintaining perfect professionalism is truly a skill. You're like a master of your craft.

jokes.text: Guess I'll just head over to the mall then, buy Erin some sexy, fun outfits. That Clark, huh?
jokes.setup: Pete sent to buy Erin clothes
jokes.punchline: Pete's uncomfortable realization about the situation
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Pete's dawning awareness of being manipulated

dialogue.speaker: Pete
dialogue.line: Wait, so you want me to go shopping for... women's clothes? For Erin?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Yes, Pete. She needs a new wardrobe.

dialogue.speaker: Pete
dialogue.line: But I don't... I mean, I've never really... How am I supposed to know what size she is?

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: I'm a size 6!

dialogue.speaker: Pete
dialogue.line: Oh God. I'm going to a women's store alone. Everyone's going to think I'm... they're all going to look at me like I'm some kind of... This is not happening.

jokes.text: What is the craziest place you ever made whoopee? Language. Who was Pam's first celebrity crush?
jokes.punchline: Kevin's inappropriate sexual question corrected
jokes.explanation: Standard Kevin inappropriate content - sexual question at family event

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: If you could have any superpower, what would it be?

dialogue.speaker: Unknown character
dialogue.line: That's not the question, Kevin.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Oh right, sorry. Um... if you could have sex with any celebrity, who would it be?

dialogue.speaker: Unknown character
dialogue.line: Kevin, no. The question is: 'If you could have dinner with anyone, living or dead, who would it be?'

jokes.text: John Stamos was temporary. I quickly moved on to Johnny... Johnny Depp.
jokes.punchline: Pam's pattern of Johnny-named celebrities
jokes.explanation: Mild character revelation - Pam's celebrity type preference

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Wait, they're all named Johnny! Johnny Depp, Johnny Knoxville, Johnny Cash...

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Pam, that's just a coincidence.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Is it though? Is it?

jokes.text: Um, I was having a separate conversation with Kevin?
jokes.punchline: Pam's awkward clarification about private conversation
jokes.explanation: Social awkwardness - Pam navigating multiple conversation streams

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: That was a private conversation between me and Jim.

jokes.text: I think maybe they're actually is something I don't know about Jim.
jokes.punchline: Pam's growing suspicion about secrets
jokes.explanation: Good character development - Pam's dawning awareness of Jim's deception

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Jim, who keeps calling you? You're always sneaking away to take these mysterious calls.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Oh, it's nothing. Just work stuff.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Work stuff? Jim, we work together. I see your calls come through the office phone all the time.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Okay, okay. It's a surprise.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: A surprise? For who?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: For you.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Oh my God. Oh my God, are you...

jokes.text: Andy made me his consigliere, which means assistant regional manager. I guess he thought I'd be into The Godfather because I'm black. Wrong! I'm into The Godfather 'cause I'm a cinephile.
jokes.punchline: Corrects racial assumption with film expertise
jokes.explanation: Excellent character work - Darryl addresses stereotypes while asserting intellectual identity

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: I'm not just some guy who knows about urban culture. I have a degree in film studies.

jokes.text: I like Scarface 'cause I'm black.
jokes.punchline: Immediately embraces different racial stereotype
jokes.explanation: Brilliant callback and subversion - immediately contradicts his own point with perfect timing

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: Yeah, I appreciate that. But you know what? I'm actually really into nascar.

jokes.text: Why not her credibility or her reliability?
jokes.setup: Discussion about Erin's appearance for news
jokes.punchline: Oscar's logical journalism standards
jokes.explanation: Classic Oscar - brings rational analysis to absurd situation

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: You can't put Erin on the news. She looks like a sexy muppet.

jokes.text: Cronkite was hot. If I could go back in time, I'd take that moustache ride.
jokes.punchline: Sexual innuendo about news anchor mustache
jokes.explanation: Outrageous Andy moment - combines news history with inappropriate sexual reference

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Walter Cronkite had an incredible mustache. That thing was sexual.

jokes.text: I will do whatever it takes to get the job. Whatever it takes.
jokes.punchline: Ominous repetition suggesting compromise
jokes.explanation: Dark setup for Erin's potential exploitation - repetition adds ominous tone

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: I'm going to get that news job. I'm going to get that news job. I'm going to get that news job.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: I'm going to get A news job. I'm going to get A news job.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: I'm going to get news. I'm going to get news.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: I'm going to get... a job.

jokes.text: Afghan president Hamid Karzai declared a new policy of dollar days throughout the country, promising low, low prices on all 2012 Kia Sentras and Sonatas.
jokes.punchline: Afghanistan car dealership promotion
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist news - combines serious geopolitics with car dealership advertising

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: We go now live to Afghanistan where a local car dealership is having a huge going out of business sale. Prices have been slashed up to 60% off. When we return, a look at some of the deals they're offering. Back to you in the studio.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Erin, Afghanistan doesn't have car dealerships.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Well, not anymore.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: No, I mean... they never did.

jokes.text: A little bit here, a little bit there. I bet you didn't think I knew current events.
jokes.setup: Clark asks about ridiculous news source
jokes.punchline: Erin's proud ignorance of journalism standards
jokes.explanation: Perfect Erin - treats made-up news as impressive research skills

dialogue.speaker: Clark
dialogue.line: Wait, where did you hear that?

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: I read it on the internet.

dialogue.speaker: Clark
dialogue.line: On what website?

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: I don't know, some website. Does it matter?

jokes.text: Pause after news. For Channel 11 News, I'm Erin Hannon. No, pause longer.
jokes.punchline: Obsessive focus on meaningless pause timing
jokes.explanation: Effective cringe - Clark's fake professionalism with pointless direction

dialogue.speaker: Clark
dialogue.line: No, no, no. You need to pause after 'meaningless.' Let me show you.

dialogue.speaker: Clark
dialogue.line: Obsessive focus on... meaningless... pause.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Like that?

dialogue.speaker: Clark
dialogue.line: No, the pause needs to be longer. Obsessive focus on meaningless... ...pause.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: I don't think I'm getting this.

dialogue.speaker: Clark
dialogue.line: It's all about the timing. Obsessive. Focus. On. Meaningless. Pause.

jokes.text: I ran into this guy from my high school who has just gotten divorced, and he hit on me in the mall. And I didn't tell you because... I don't know, I felt embarrassed, and I didn't know if you'd be mad or worried, but anyway, I thought you'd wanna know.
jokes.setup: Pam trying to share something Jim doesn't know
jokes.punchline: Awkward confession about being hit on
jokes.explanation: Authentic marital awkwardness - Pam's unnecessary confession creates discomfort

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Jim, there's something I need to tell you. A guy hit on me today at lunch.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: What? Who?

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: I don't know, just some guy. But I didn't encourage it or anything.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Okay... well, I mean, you're attractive, so...

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: That's sweet, but also weird that you're okay with it?

jokes.text: That didn't happen. You would've told me right away. Yeah, I would've.
jokes.setup: Pam's made-up story about being hit on
jokes.punchline: Jim immediately calls out the lie
jokes.explanation: Great couple dynamic - Jim knows Pam too well for her to fabricate stories

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: This guy at the coffee shop asked me out. He said I had the most beautiful eyes he'd ever seen.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Pam, you're wearing sunglasses.

jokes.text: God, give it up, Beesly. You know me too well.
jokes.punchline: Jim's defeated admission with maiden name usage
jokes.explanation: Sweet character moment - Jim's affectionate surrender using old nickname

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: What aren't you telling me?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Fine. Okay. I've been going to the same haircut place since high school.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: That's not a secret.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: The stylist's name is Debra Halpert.

jokes.text: Now, I'd say Clark could be your co-host. He's already doing camera. Somebody who's already got rapport with Erin, maybe. I don't know.
jokes.punchline: Obliviously enables Clark's scheme
jokes.explanation: Frustrating dramatic irony - Andy unknowingly helps Clark's predatory plan

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Erin, you need a co-host. Who do you think would be good?

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Clark!

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Clark is a great choice. You two will be perfect together.

dialogue.speaker: Clark
dialogue.line: Thanks, Andy.

jokes.text: 127 Hours. It's about this guy who... No, no, no, no, no spoilers. Please.
jokes.punchline: Dwight's dramatic anti-spoiler reaction
jokes.explanation: Good character contrast - Dwight's intense spoiler sensitivity vs. Darryl's casual attitude

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: I brought in this motivational movie for everyone to watch.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. Does the hero die?

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: I'm not telling you.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: If you don't tell me right now, I will leave this office and never watch your movie.

jokes.text: Well, he tried to, but the fire door was blocked. He sure did.
jokes.setup: Pete's kitchen fire cliché about heat
jokes.punchline: Erin's literal interpretation of failed escape
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark comedy - Erin turns cliché into grim reality about blocked exits

dialogue.speaker: Pete
dialogue.line: Yeah, it's so hot in here, we need to get out of the kitchen.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: But I already tried the window and it's painted shut!

jokes.text: I could do a couple more takes. We could do it in close-up. That might even be better for editing.
jokes.setup: Pete trying to continue fake audition
jokes.punchline: Desperate attempts to seem professional while sabotaging Clark
jokes.explanation: Good character work - Pete's protective instincts disguised as professional suggestions

dialogue.speaker: Pete
dialogue.line: So, uh, I've been doing this for about five years now, very professional, very committed to the craft.

dialogue.speaker: Pete
dialogue.line: I trained at Juilliard... well, I watched videos about Juilliard.

dialogue.speaker: Pete
dialogue.line: And I think what really sets me apart is my dedication and my ability to stay focused under pressure, unlike some people who just crumble.

dialogue.speaker: Pete
dialogue.line: Speaking of which, my colleague Clark over there, he's actually been really struggling with his confidence lately. He told me he doesn't think he has what it takes.

dialogue.speaker: Pete
dialogue.line: But me? I'm ready for anything. Prime material. Star quality. The complete package.

jokes.text: So this is a single. Hey, even this doesn't work out for me, I'm just glad I had the guts to do it, and maybe it'll work out for Andy.
jokes.punchline: Still doesn't realize she was being exploited
jokes.explanation: Bittersweet character moment - Erin's optimism despite exploitation attempt

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: I learned so much from working here. Kelly taught me about fashion, Ryan taught me about business, and Dwight taught me about... I don't know, beet farming I guess. And you know what? I wouldn't change a single thing about any of it.

jokes.text: Well, he's a genius, you know? He was in graduate programs at Yale, Columbia and NYU all at the same time. Whoop-de-doo. That doesn't make you a genius.
jokes.punchline: Dwight's dismissive response to impressive credentials
jokes.explanation: Good character contrast - Nellie's celebrity worship vs. Dwight's contrarian nature

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: James Franco went to Yale and he's a Rhodes Scholar.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: False. He went to high school.

jokes.text: You're the stupid one. Like you. You, you, you, you, you...
jokes.setup: Dwight and Nellie arguing about intelligence
jokes.punchline: Devolves into childish back-and-forth
jokes.explanation: Standard escalation ending - intelligent characters reduced to playground insults

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I have an IQ of 161. What's yours, Nellie?

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: Higher than your emotional intelligence, that's for certain.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Oh, that's rich coming from someone who can't even pronounce 'charcuterie' correctly.

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: At least I don't tuck my shirts into my pants like some sort of overgrown toddler.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: This is proper German fashion, Nellie. Not that you'd understand European culture.

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: I'm British!

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Exactly my point.

tmdb_overview: A toast at Roy's wedding leads to Pam and Jim searching their relationships for buried secrets; Dwight reacts to Nellie's mandatory charity initiative.
insight: Wedding chaos spawns 77 jokes in 42 minutes—character comedy sustains the density surge.
